title: Personal Jurisdiction
summary: How a court obtains power over a defendant, including traditional bases, long-arm analysis, and minimum contacts.
learningObjectives: Separate statutory authorization from constitutional due process.
Identify when general versus specific jurisdiction applies.
Analyze purposeful availment and fairness factors.

rules.title: Traditional Bases
rules.blackLetterLaw: A court has personal jurisdiction when service is proper and a traditional basis applies, such as in-state service, domicile, or consent.
rules.explanation: Traditional Pennoyer-era bases remain valid shortcuts to personal jurisdiction even before a modern contacts analysis is necessary.
elements: Tag jurisdiction by personal service while physically present in the forum
Express or implied consent, including forum-selection clauses and failure to timely object
pitfalls: Treating transient service as enough for entities in all circumstances

rules.title: Minimum Contacts Framework
rules.blackLetterLaw: When no traditional basis applies, due process requires minimum contacts such that exercising jurisdiction does not offend traditional notions of fair play and substantial justice.
rules.explanation: The defendant must purposefully create a meaningful connection with the forum, and the exercise of jurisdiction must be fair.
elements: Purposeful availment or purposeful direction toward the forum
Foreseeability of being haled into court there
Fairness factors such as burden, forum interest, plaintiff interest, and efficiency
pitfalls: Relying only on the plaintiff's forum contacts
Skipping the fairness analysis after finding contacts

rules.title: Specific vs. General Jurisdiction
rules.blackLetterLaw: General jurisdiction allows any claim when the defendant is essentially at home in the forum, while specific jurisdiction requires a claim arising out of or relating to the defendant's forum contacts.
rules.explanation: General jurisdiction is broad but rare. Specific jurisdiction is narrower and claim-linked.
elements: General jurisdiction usually where an individual is domiciled or a corporation is incorporated or has its principal place of business
Specific jurisdiction requires a nexus between the controversy and the forum contacts
The defendant's own conduct must tie the dispute to the forum
pitfalls: Calling a large volume of national business enough for general jurisdiction everywhere
Ignoring the claim-contact relationship for specific jurisdiction

questions.prompt: A defendant from State A is personally served while attending a conference in State B. The lawsuit concerns an unrelated contract dispute. What is the strongest basis for State B personal jurisdiction?

choices.text: General jurisdiction because the defendant travels nationally
Tag jurisdiction based on in-state personal service
Specific jurisdiction because the plaintiff sued there
No jurisdiction because the claim is unrelated to State B

questions.rationale: Personal service on an individual while physically present in the forum is a classic traditional basis for personal jurisdiction.

questions.prompt: A manufacturer deliberately markets and ships the product at issue into the forum state, where the injury occurs. Which jurisdiction theory best fits?

choices.text: Specific jurisdiction based on forum-directed contacts tied to the claim
General jurisdiction because products entered the state
No jurisdiction unless the defendant is domiciled there
Jurisdiction only if the plaintiff resides there

questions.rationale: Purposeful shipment and marketing into the forum tied to the injury usually support specific jurisdiction.
